periods.name: Today
periods.short: Chance Rain Showers then Slight Chance Showers And Thunderstorms
periods.detailed: A chance of rain showers between 11am and 1pm, then a slight chance of showers and thunderstorms between 4pm and 5pm, then a chance of showers and thunderstorms. Partly sunny, with a high near 78. South wind 15 to 18 mph, with gusts as high as 35 mph. Chance of precipitation is 50%. New rainfall amounts between a tenth and quarter of an inch possible.

periods.name: Tonight
periods.detailed: Showers and thunderstorms. Mostly cloudy, with a low around 66. South wind 12 to 16 mph, with gusts as high as 30 mph. Chance of precipitation is 80%. New rainfall amounts between a quarter and half of an inch possible.

periods.name: Wednesday
periods.detailed: A chance of rain showers between 10am and noon, then a chance of showers and thunderstorms between noon and 1pm, then a slight chance of showers and thunderstorms between 1pm and 2pm. Mostly sunny, with a high near 81. Southwest wind around 13 mph. Chance of precipitation is 30%. New rainfall amounts less than a tenth of an inch possible.

periods.name: Wednesday Night
periods.detailed: Partly cloudy, with a low around 57. West wind 6 to 13 mph.

periods.name: Thursday
periods.detailed: Sunny, with a high near 78. West wind around 8 mph.

periods.name: Thursday Night
periods.detailed: Mostly clear, with a low around 58. Southwest wind 2 to 7 mph.

key_messages: Multiple rounds of showers and storms today and tomorrow.
Blue sky conditions to end the week with cool and crisp air.
Quiet and hot weather is expected this weekend before another storm system brings showers and thunderstorms to the region early next week.
afd.what_changed: As of 249 AM EDT Tuesday... No significant changes.
afd.raw:
000
FXUS61 KBTV 211112
AFDBTV

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Burlington VT
712 AM EDT Tue Jul 21 2026

.WHAT HAS CHANGED...
As of 249 AM EDT Tuesday...

No significant changes.

&&

.KEY MESSAGES...
As of 249 AM EDT Tuesday...

1. Multiple rounds of showers and storms today and tomorrow.

2. Blue sky conditions to end the week with cool and crisp air.

3. Quiet and hot weather is expected this weekend before 
another storm system brings showers and thunderstorms to the region 
early next week.

&&

.DISCUSSION...
As of 249 AM EDT Tuesday...

KEY MESSAGE 1: An occluding low will track well to the north today into 
Wednesday, bringing several rounds of showers. A few showers will 
move through this morning, but more widespread showers and 
thunderstorms will move through in the afternoon and evening. They 
will mostly dissipate and move out tonight, before another round of 
showers and thunderstorms develops on Wednesday, though mostly for 
Vermont. Right now, the severe threat is relatively low. There does 
not look to be a well-defined boundary; instead several rounds of 
showers with plenty of clouds will move through. There is very 
little time for solar heating and instability to form, and the 
airmass will be relatively cool to start with. With the low tracking 
well to the north, the best synoptic forcing will be well to the 
north, though there will be adequate wind shear. Overall, the region 
is in an SPC marginal risk which seems representative. While an 
isolated strong to severe storm is possible, that currently looks to 
be the extent of it, though the amount of heating will still be 
watched as more sun than expected could increase storm coverage a 
little. The system does not look to provide much of a flooding 
threat either. HREF probabilities of receiving more than an inch of 
rain are generally between 10-40 percent. While there will be areas 
of convective enhancement, the storms will be quick-moving and there 
will be gaps between the showers. PWATs should rise into the 1.5-
1.75 inch range but with the expected setup, that should not pose 
too much of a problem. While limited wildfire smoke may return 
today, its concentration will be significantly less than on Saturday 
and it will likely not have much of an effect on the convection.

KEY MESSAGE 2: A seasonably cool air mass, with minimal high altitude 
smoke, should make for outstanding weather for outdoor activities on 
Thursday and Friday as an upper level trough pushes to our 
northeast. Westerly flow in the morning will probably support some 
rough conditions on Lake Champlain that should improve during the 
day as anomalous low level temperatures moderate along with 
decreasing wind speeds. Winds decrease significantly for Friday. 
Expect high temperatures not too far off of seasonable norms due to 
abundant sunshine, currently projected to range through the 70s and 
into the lower 80s. Lows at night will be in the 40s and 50s.

KEY MESSAGE 3: High pressure will dominate this weekend, resulting in 
dry weather Friday night into Sunday. This is also allow for above 
seasonable temperatures with highs in the 80s and lows in the 50s to 
mid 60s as southwesterly flow aloft increases, advecting in warmer 
air ahead of our next system. This next system is expected to swing 
through early next week featuring a trough and cold front. This far 
out, models show significant differences from each other as well as 
from run to run, but most likely period of showers and thunderstorms 
would be Monday. This should also knock temperatures back down 
towards normal in the 70s to mid 80 and lows in the upper 40s to 
lower 60s.

&&

.AVIATION /12Z TUESDAY THROUGH SATURDAY/...
Through 12Z Wednesday...VFR conditions will likely prevail at
all sites this morning through around 19Z-23Z Tuesday, though
multiple rounds of showers and storms are anticipated to come
through northern New York and Vermont today and tomorrow. At
this time, probability of thunderstorms hitting a given terminal 
are around 20-40%, most likely at SLK and MSS during the time
frame 17Z-21Z Tuesday. Any thunderstorm may produce gusty to
strong winds and briefly heavy rain/low visibilities. Otherwise,
on and off showers are likely with overall winds out of the
south gusting up to 20-35 knots this afternoon. A strong
south-southwesterly low level jet increases potential for LLWS 
throughout the day at several terminals, including BTV, SLK, 
PBG, and RUT. With the arrival of showers and thunderstorms,
ceilings may dip to MVFR levels briefly, but a more widespread
and longer-lasting lowering of ceilings should arrive later 
this evening and tonight. Cloud layers between 500 and 3500 
feet above ground level are likely toward 00Z Wednesday and 
onward, with vicinity patchy fog wafting through the valleys.
Wind likely stays too elevated overnight for dense, stuck fog,
but plenty of moisture will be around to make the forecast
tricky. 


Outlook...

Wednesday: Mainly VFR, with local MVFR possible. Chance SHRA,
Slight chance TSRA.
Wednesday Night: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Thursday: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Thursday Night: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Friday: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Friday Night: VFR. NO SIG WX.
Saturday: VFR. NO SIG WX.

&&

.MARINE...
A Lake Wind Advisory is expected to be in effect through 
tonight. Southerly winds will quickly increase in the latter
portion of the night and by morning, sustained winds are
expected to be in the 20-30 KT range. These winds stay elevated
for the rest of the day and the evening. Winds will eventually
become westerly and decrease for late tonight and tomorrow, 
dropping into the 10-20 KT range. Multiple rounds of showers and
thunderstorms are expected today, with stronger and erratic 
winds possible in any of those. The highest coverage of showers 
and storms will be in the afternoon and evening. Waves will 
quickly build into the 2-4 FT range for today and this evening, 
before eventually dropping into the 1-2 FT range late in the 
night.

&&

.BTV WATCHES/WARNINGS/ADVISORIES...
VT...None.
NY...None.

&&

$$

WHAT HAS CHANGED...Myskowski
DISCUSSION...Storm/Myskowski
AVIATION...Storm
MARINE...NWS BTV

air.discussion: On Tuesday, July 21st, expect a chance for some low-moderate fine particulate matter pollution if a trace of wildfire smoke mixes into the area from the south.
